segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black screen, transitioning to a bold red 'FRONTLINE' title card over a cityscape, accompanied by ominous, dramatic orchestral music. A deep, authoritative male narrator announces, 'Tonight on Frontline. Episode one of a special four-hour investigation.' The scene shifts to an urban protest with individuals holding signs like 'WALL ST. IS WAR ST.' and 'FILTHY RICH,' amidst protest sounds and an angry male voice shouting, 'You created the mess we're in, and now you're saying, sorry, trust us.' This is followed by a formal hearing with men in suits at a long table with microphones, as a fast-paced male voice mentions 'as much as 50 million dollars a year.' The narrative then moves to a financial trading floor, showing traders intensely focused on multiple computer screens displaying stock data, with close-ups of hands typing and monitors flashing. The narrator asks, 'Inside the financial crisis... How did we get here?' while a calm, analytical male voice states, 'Wall Street got bailed out and Main Street didn't.' A woman in an orange top then speaks directly to the camera, providing commentary in a calm, explanatory female voice, explaining, 'All the banks were taking these ideas and applying them in ways that they'd never expected. Once the seed was planted, there wasn't any stopping it.' Another male voice reflects, 'We never imagined. They were just taking the risk,' followed by an emphatic male voice adding, 'And it came right back like a boomerang.' The video cuts to a bustling office environment, then a close-up of a 'WALL STREET' sign, as a dramatic female voice exclaims, 'They turned into a Frankenstein monster.' The sequence continues with a high-angle shot of a city skyline, then a train speeding past a platform marked 'To New York,' symbolizing movement toward the financial hub. The male narrator concludes, 'Money, power, and Wall Street. Episode one. Tonight on Frontline,' as the ominous music swells and fades, replaced by distinct and intensifying train sounds, including rumbling, clattering, and a horn, as the train is shown in motion, emphasizing transit and connectivity to New York City.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a dynamic shot of a silver and red Train to New York speeding past a platform marked 'To New York,' accompanied by the distinct sounds of a train rushing and clattering. An authoritative male narrator begins, stating, "Every day, tens of thousands of workers make their way to Wall Street." The camera shifts to an overhead view of the train tracks as the train moves through an urban landscape, with the narrator detailing, "They work for banks, brokerages, hedge funds, insurance companies, and mortgage lenders." Inside the bustling train, passengers are seen reading newspapers—one with a 'MONEY & INVESTING' headline—and using phones, reflecting the daily commute. The city skyline blurs past the train window as the narrator emphasizes Wall Street's scale: "It is the largest single sector of the American economy. An industry that is almost double the size of America's manufacturing sector." A dramatic, suspenseful musical score begins to build as a title card appears with bold red text: 'MONEY, POWER AND WALL STREET — A RISKY BUSINESS,' perfectly aligning with the narrator's declaration that it's "A business with enormous power and global reach. It is the industry that led America and the world into its worst economic crisis since the Great Depression." Production credits for Martin Smith, Marcela Gaviria, Thomas Jennings, Linda Hirsch, and Ben Gold are displayed over the moving cityscape, with the intense music continuing. The scene concludes at a subway station with a 'Wall Street' sign as a train rushes past, reinforcing the financial district's central role, while the narrator adds, "The banks say they exist to create wealth, holding in trust our collective worth, promising to invest the trillions..." The lighting shifts from daylight to dusk, creating a moody atmosphere that underscores the themes of power and risk.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dynamic shot of a subway train speeding past the 'Wall Street' station sign, as red production credits appear. A male narrator's voice, accompanied by somber, reflective background music, speaks about "dollars that stream in from businesses, pension funds, and savings accounts that belong to all of us." The scene transitions to a bustling street-level protest where a diverse group of Protesters march with signs such as 'WALL ST. IS WAR ST.', 'FILTHY RICH', and 'EAT THE RICH', expressing anger toward Wall Street and corporate power. Loud protest chants of "We got sold out! We got sold out!" and a distinct voice shouting "You must go on the sidewalk!" fill the air. Police officers in uniform are seen managing the crowd, with a news van marked '4 HD' indicating media presence. The narrator explains, "One morning in the fall of 2011, bankers arriving in Lower Manhattan were caught by surprise." Close-ups capture individuals holding signs, including one reading 'TOO BIG TO FAIL HAS FAILED', underscoring financial crisis and public discontent. The narrator continues, "The recession had destroyed 11 trillion dollars of Americans' net worth. A recovery seemed far off. Occupy Wall Street wanted bankers held responsible." The protest moves toward a grand building, likely the New York Stock Exchange, with American flags visible. The scene cuts to an interview with David Wessel from The Wall Street Journal, identified by a lower-third graphic, who states, "Most Americans think, and with good reason, that Wall Street got bailed out and Main Street didn't. We have very high unemployment. We lost eight and a half million jobs in the recession." A voice shouts "Please join in!" The video then returns to the protest, showing a man in a black shirt raising a cardboard sign toward a crowd behind barricades, followed by a wide shot of demonstrators chanting and waving signs in front of the iconic building. David Wessel's voice continues, explaining, "People's houses aren't worth what they paid for them. A lot of them don't have jobs. Their kids are graduating from college and are moving back in," as the charged atmosphere of civic unrest and financial critique is underscored by the ongoing protest chants and crowd noise.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Dennis Kelleher, a financial reform advocate, speaking earnestly in a dimly lit interview setting, wearing glasses, a dark suit, and a green-striped tie, asserting that there was "massive illegality" and that criminal prosecutions would be "quite successful." The scene then transitions to a dynamic protest at Wall Street, where a large crowd of demonstrators, holding signs like 'WALL ST. IS WAR ST.' and 'TOO BIG TO FAIL HAS FAILED,' loudly chants "We want justice!" Police officers stand guard, and a news van marked '4 HD' is visible, indicating media coverage. A male narrator, identified as FRONTLINE correspondent Martin Smith, explains that in a matter of weeks, "Occupy demonstrations" spread to scores of cities, calling for "radical changes in the banking system." The camera pans across the energetic protest under grand arches, then shifts to an aerial view of New York City’s dense urban landscape before moving inside a modern, empty boardroom overlooking the city. The narrator continues, noting that "bankers responded by saying that the answer is to move on and get back to business." The video cuts to Steve Bartlett, identified as part of the Financial Services Roundtable, speaking in a formal interview setting, acknowledging that "some Wall Street companies made a series of bad mistakes" for which "we all paid for them," leading to the "economic crisis." Finally, Martin Smith is seen in conversation in front of a bookshelf, asking what makes people upset, specifically referencing "a lot of the people that are on the streets demonstrating, Occupy Wall Street," juxtaposing grassroots activism with corporate and financial authority.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with FRONTLINE correspondent Martin Smith, in a dark suit before a bookshelf, stating that while the "economy hasn't recovered, banks have." The scene transitions to Steve Bartlett of the Financial Services Roundtable, in a suit with a red patterned tie and an American flag pin, seated in an office, gesturing as he emphatically argues that a strong economy requires "safe and sound" financial services companies, warning that if banks are "hammered" and "fail," it will lead to "another recession." Tense, dramatic background music then underscores the purposeful walk of Dennis Kelleher, a financial reform advocate in a green-striped tie, and other suited men through a government hallway, flanked by media personnel with cameras. They enter a crowded, tense hearing room, filled with the clamor of reporters shouting questions at figures like Lloyd Blankfein, a bald man in a dark suit and blue tie, seated at a hearing table. Reporters demand to know if he understands "why they're angry" and if he has "any regrets about the way you've spent the taxpayers' money," to which a male voice, presumably Blankfein's, firmly replies, "Absolutely not." Brooksley Born, a woman with short brown hair in a pinstripe blazer, appearing emotional and distressed, is also seen at a nameplate. The scene returns to Martin Smith, who narrates that "since the meltdown of 2008, there have been dozens of hearings." A male voice then expresses regret that "people have lost money," acknowledging that "whatever we did, whatever the standards of the time were, it didn't work out well." Finally, Brooksley Born, still seen at a hearing table, is heard asking for an opinion on "the role that over the counter derivatives play."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a formal hearing setting, where a male voice (narrator) sets the tone by stating that "questions have been asked in contributing to the financial crisis" but there have been "few satisfying answers." John Mack, a man in a dark suit and tie, is seen seated seriously and attentively at a table with his nameplate. The scene cuts to a man in a dark suit and green-striped tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting, posing questions like "What goes on at Wall Street and exactly what caused the crisis and how did we get where we are? It's difficult to understand, even for professionals." John Mack then speaks into the microphone with an earnest expression, stating, "I'm not sure I understand that point. Maybe you could elaborate." Lloyd Blankfein, a bald man in a dark suit and red patterned tie, is shown at a hearing table with his nameplate, speaking into a microphone with a concerned expression, explaining, "Well, I think it's uh in many ways it's very simple. I think our regulators and the industry have to focus on complexity." The green-striped tie interviewee returns, commenting, "But at the end of the day, people usually have a pretty good ability to tell when something's wrong." Byron Georgiou, Commissioner, in a pinstripe suit and glasses, speaks at his hearing table, reflecting, "somehow we just missed uh, you know, that home prices don't go up forever." A brief shot shows a man (Dennis Kelleher) in a dark suit walking through a government hallway, his face partially obscured. James Dimon, a man with gray hair in a dark suit, is seen composed at his hearing table, asking, "What is a synthetic CDO?" Lloyd Blankfein then speaks animatedly, gesturing with his hand, his face showing intensity as he clarifies, "A CDO is a pool of assets." The video shifts to a man in a dark suit and blue tie, speaking in a dimly lit interview setting with a serious and focused expression, stating, "I think finance may have gotten too complicated for anyone to understand." Lloyd Blankfein continues his explanation, detailing how assets "are pooled together and then can be sliced in a synthetic, you pool reference securities that are indexed to specific pools of mortgages." The blue-tie interviewee concludes, mentioning "And that the managers..." The overall atmosphere is tense and formal, emphasizing the gravity of the financial and regulatory discussions.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Frank Partnoy, identified by a 'FRONTLINE' lower-third graphic as 'Morgan Stanley, 1994-95', speaking earnestly in a dimly lit interview setting. He calmly but seriously states that large financial institutions have been given an "impossible task" and can't comprehend their own actions, calling it "really, really scary." The scene then transitions to a formal hearing room where Committee Members in suits listen intently. A key figure, a man in a dark suit with a striped tie and a U.S. House pin, speaks assertively into a microphone, accusing them of creating the mess, CDOs, and credit default swaps, demanding, "Find him, fire him!" The atmosphere shifts dramatically to a tranquil tropical setting, with a silhouette of palm fronds against a vibrant sunset, accompanied by the distinct sound of crickets chirping. A male narrator's voice begins, explaining that while it's "hard to pinpoint the origins of America's financial crisis," "one weekend at this resort in Boca Raton, Florida, is a good place to start." Soft, upbeat background music starts as wide shots reveal a grand pink hotel surrounded by palm trees under a clear blue sky. The camera pans to a luxurious poolside area with lounge chairs and blue umbrellas, where a woman in a bikini lounges and another person holds a red drink. The narrator continues, noting that a group of young bankers from JP Morgan assembled here in June 1994, and "at the time, it all seemed innocent enough," with faint chatter and laughter blending with the relaxed poolside visuals and music.

The scene transitions to a formal hearing room where a group of men in suits, seated at a long table with microphones and nameplates, listen intently—these are the Committee Members. The camera focuses on one man in a dark suit with a striped tie and a U.S. House pin, who speaks into a microphone, suggesting he is a key figure in the proceedings. The scene then shifts dramatically to a tropical setting, opening with a silhouette of palm fronds against a vibrant sunset, followed by wide shots of a grand pink hotel surrounded by palm trees under a clear blue sky. The camera pans to a poolside area with lounge chairs and blue umbrellas, where a woman in a bikini lounges by the pool, her legs visible, and another person holds a red drink with a straw. The atmosphere transitions from serious governmental inquiry to a relaxed, luxurious vacation environment, emphasizing a stark contrast in tone and setting.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a serene, silhouetted scene of two men seated at a poolside table during sunset, their forms dark against the glowing sky and distant cityscape, suggesting a relaxed, private conversation. Over this, Blythe Masters' voice begins, describing the retreat as a gathering of JP Morgan's global derivative group, for celebration, relaxation, and, more importantly, to bring creative, innovative people together to discuss various topics. A close-up of a plastic cup labeled 'Resort & Club' with condensation hints at a tropical resort setting. The scene transitions to an interview with Blythe Masters, identified by a lower-third graphic as 'BLYTHE MASTERS, JP Morgan, 1991-present,' wearing a white blazer and pink scarf, speaking with a serious expression. The video cuts to a blurred shot of a poolside lounge chair as Gillian Tett's voice begins, recounting how the young attendees, mostly in their 20s with plenty of money and high spirits, did what any young bunch of kids would do. The scene then shifts to Gillian Tett, identified as 'GILLIAN TETT, Author, Fool's Gold,' in an orange top, speaking directly to the camera, detailing how they got drunk, had parties, and threw each other in pools, characterizing it as 'normal stuff that happens at conferences.' A close-up of a hand holding a drink is shown, followed by a swimmer in a pool, with lounge chairs and people in the background, as Bill Winters' voice states he went into the pool fully clothed, as did his boss. The next interview features Bill Winters, labeled 'BILL WINTERS, JP Morgan, 1983-09,' in a striped shirt and tie, speaking with a composed demeanor, adding that while some people drank, most stayed 'reasonably sober.' The video concludes with wide, tranquil shots of a well-lit pool at dusk, surrounded by palm trees and resort buildings, emphasizing a luxurious, calm atmosphere, as a dramatic, slightly melancholic instrumental track plays and a male narrator explains that the attendees 'played hard, but they also worked hard,' striving to address the 'age-old problem in banking: how to reduce risk.'

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a serene nighttime scene at a tropical resort, focusing on a poolside area illuminated by warm lights. A close-up reveals a blue 'POOL RULES AND REGULATIONS' sign and a white 'BOCA RESORT' lifebuoy, as gentle, melancholic ambient music plays. The camera pans across rows of empty lounge chairs, closed blue umbrellas, palm trees, and distant city lights, creating a deserted yet tranquil atmosphere. The scene transitions to an indoor interview setting where a woman in an orange top, identified by a male narrator as Gillian Tett, speaks expressively. Gillian Tett's clear, articulate voice explains how financial institutions sought to 'pass risk between them' by selling loans or separating 'the risk of a loan going bad from the loan itself,' leading to the development of 'credit default swaps.' The video then abruptly cuts to a chaotic stock exchange floor, where traders in yellow jackets are intensely engaged, shouting and gesturing amid flashing red and green digital tickers. Loud, bustling crowd noise and indistinct shouting fill the audio as the camera sweeps across the frenetic, paper-strewn trading floor. The male narrator's voice returns over the din, defining 'Credit default swaps' as 'a kind of derivative that insures a loan against default,' and elaborating on their historical use by farmers to protect against fluctuating crop prices, and their current trading on the commodities exchange in Chicago for fuels, currencies, and precious metals.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with chaotic, high-angle shots of a crowded trading floor, where intensely engaged Traders shout and gesture amidst monitors, capturing the frenetic energy of financial markets. A male voice explains that the JP Morgan team realized they could use credit derivatives to trade their loan risks. The scene shifts to Gillian Tett, a woman in an orange top, speaking expressively in an interview setting, her articulate voice explaining how banks borrowed ideas from the commodities market and applied them to loans for the first time, under the banner of making the financial system safer. Archival footage then shows a woman in a dark pinstripe suit, likely Brooksley Born, at a desk with an old computer, as a narrator details that the first big credit default swap was engineered by Blythe Masters and involved Exxon. A female voice adds that Exxon was the client of the bank, with credit exposure associated with that relationship. The narrative transitions to a serene natural landscape before cutting to a large oil tanker at sea, accompanied by the sound of a helicopter and a news report detailing how the Exxon Valdez spewed almost 11 million gallons of oil into Prince William Sound. This leads to a person in an orange suit handling an oil-covered bird on a rocky shore, emphasizing environmental consequences. The narrator continues, referencing the Exxon Valdez oil spill and a rash of lawsuits, with a news reporter adding that a five-count indictment was returned. The video then shows a man in a suit and cap walking through a city street, followed by Office Workers carrying briefcases, as the narrator reveals Exxon took out a multi-billion dollar letter of credit with JP Morgan. A news reporter calls this a "sweeping blow for big business," before the female voice from earlier concludes that a letter of credit creates credit risk if Exxon were to default, as the final shots show a towering skyscraper and the entrance to the JPMorgan building at 60 Wall Street.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a chaotic, high-angle shot of a trading floor, where Traders are intensely focused on multiple computer screens displaying financial data, their hands typing rapidly, capturing the frenetic energy of a busy market environment. Over this, a female voice, likely from the 'Woman in Orange Top' entity, narrates with a clear, informative tone, explaining that if companies defaulted on their obligations, JP Morgan would have to step in and make good on those obligations on their behalf, noting it was a large amount of exposure and a significant amount of risk. The scene quickly shifts to a close-up of a woman with red hair, wearing a white blouse and a pink patterned scarf, her hand covering her mouth in a gesture of concern or shock. This is followed by a low-angle shot of a towering skyscraper under a cloudy sky, with an American flag waving in the wind, emphasizing the scale and power of financial institutions. The narrative then transitions to an interview with Satyajit Das, identified by a lower-third graphic as a 'Fmr. derivatives trader,' who speaks animatedly in a dimly lit room, gesturing with his hands as he states, "And that risk is a big drain on a bank." The scene cuts to another interviewee, a man in a dark suit with a green-striped tie, who speaks with a serious expression, his hands moving to emphasize his points, explaining that "Every time a bank makes a loan, under banking regulations, they're required to set aside certain reserves of capital for the loan. So JP Morgan when they made the loan to Exxon would have had to set aside some capital." A different male voice then elaborates, stating that "JP Morgan has to hold certain capital relative to the size of that loan in the event the loan is not paid off at 100% as you expect," and continues, "Of course, if you don't have to do that, and you're a bank, you'd prefer not to do that, because then you can finance more freely, you can take on more debt." This commentary plays over an aerial view of a wide river with barges and industrial buildings on either side, suggesting global trade and economic activity, followed by a blurred, fast-moving street scene with a red double-decker bus and pedestrians, indicating the bustling pace of city life. Another male voice, likely Satyajit Das again, then adds, "So Masters started looking at who could take on their loan risk and free up JP Morgan's capital. She found a taker in London."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide-angle shot of a bustling city street, likely London, featuring a red double-decker bus with an advertisement about TV channels disappearing, and a black taxi passing modern office buildings, one with a curved glass facade. The camera tilts upward, emphasizing the towering urban architecture. A close-up reveals a person moving inside a dimly lit office window. The scene transitions to the entrance of the JPMorgan building at 60 Wall Street, with its name engraved above revolving doors. A tense, orchestral background score begins as a male voiceover introduces "The European Bank for Reconstruction and Development, the EBRD." Inside, Blythe Masters, identified as from JP Morgan, speaks directly to the camera in a serious tone, explaining that "EBRD would receive compensation from JP Morgan for taking on or assuming credit risk and felt that that was a good risk reward proposition." She continues, stating that "risk was essentially dispersed" and JP Morgan did this "to free up our capacity to do more business." The male voiceover then declares, "This was a major financial innovation," explaining that "Credit derivatives made it possible for a bank to skirt capital requirements." The video cuts to archival footage of a crowded 1990s trading floor, filled with traders at desks with CRT monitors and telephones, capturing frenetic energy. Satyajit Das, a former derivatives trader, is then seen speaking animatedly, his voice carrying a distinct accent as he explains that "the amount of capital that banks had to hold got less," allowing them "to create more and more credit. They could make more loans." The footage returns to the trading floor, showing a large digital ticker displaying stock transactions and wide shots of the chaotic workspace. The male voiceover concludes that "The Exxon deal was just the beginning, demonstrating that risk could be offloaded and capital freed up," adding that "JP Morgan had struck gold" and in 1998, "they decided to ramp up their credit derivatives operation." A woman in a black blazer appears, superimposed over the trading floor, speaking with hands clasped, as the voiceover mentions "another young banker."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a woman in a black blazer and white shirt, seated and speaking with her hands clasped in a softly lit, neutral-toned room, suggesting a formal interview setting. The scene transitions to Terri Duhon, a woman with dark hair and bangs, wearing a black top and a long beaded necklace, who is introduced by a narrator as having 'joined the team.' She speaks expressively in a dimly lit room with wooden paneling, explaining her job was 'to come in as a trader, and to build a credit derivative trading book, including all the risk management around the more exotic products.' The narrator then provides context, stating that 'JP Morgan had written credit swaps on single companies like Exxon,' and Duhon was asked 'to write swaps on bundles of debt.' Terri elaborates, saying 'The idea was let's put together a portfolio of credit risk, um, a portfolio of names.' The visual briefly shifts to a blurred, high-angle view of a trading floor, showing individuals intensely focused on multiple computer screens, emphasizing the fast-paced, data-driven environment, followed by a silhouette of a person at a desk against a bright window. The narrator continues, detailing that 'Her first trade was a credit default swap on 306 corporate names on JP Morgan's books.' Terri Duhon, now identified by a lower-third graphic as 'TERRI DUHON, JP Morgan, 1994-02', continues her animated explanation, stating that these '306 entities, they were very highly rated, they had very low credit risk.' A man in a dark blazer and light blue shirt then appears, speaking with a pointed gesture, explaining that the credit default swap was 'insuring JP Morgan against default by those 306 entities,' many of which were 'Fortune 500 companies.' The scene briefly shows a digital stock ticker with green and red numbers, including 'ALCOA Inc' and 'Banc of America', before returning to a close-up of Terri Duhon, who listens intently and responds with subtle hand movements, confirming 'That's correct' and adding that they were 'some of your most well-known household names,' as she begins to discuss giving investors. The overall atmosphere alternates between intimate, reflective interviews and the chaotic, high-stakes environment of financial trading, underscoring the documentary’s focus on Wall Street’s inner workings.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Terri Duhon, identified by a lower-third graphic as 'TERRI DUHON, JP Morgan, 1994-02', speaking expressively in a dimly lit room with wooden paneling, wearing a black top and a long beaded necklace, explaining in a clear, articulate female voice an opportunity to "invest in our loan portfolio." The scene transitions to a series of close-ups of financial data screens, displaying fluctuating stock prices, currency rates, and commodity indices in vibrant reds, greens, and yellows, emphasizing the fast-paced nature of trading. A man in a dark suit with a blue and white striped shirt and a purple tie appears in interview shots, his expression serious and focused, detailing in a serious male voice how "JP Morgan did a lot of work... to assemble this portfolio of loans," which can be acquired in "one easy, bite-sized piece." A distinct male narrator's voice then explains how the bank "facilitated this by slicing up the portfolio into different risk levels or tranches." The man in the dark suit continues, noting that "investors could choose how much risk they were willing to take," with some seeking "a big return on really risky stuff" and others preferring "a little return on stuff that wasn't risky at all." The narrator adds that "the bank looked to expand their business even further." The video cuts to a trading floor environment, showing blurred, high-angle shots of traders intensely focused on multiple computer screens, with one hand seen writing notes on paper. A woman in a white blazer with a pink patterned scarf, identified as Blythe Masters, speaks in an interview setting, her expression serious, proposing in a clear, articulate female voice the idea of creating "a market where people were able to buy and sell freely, independently of the companies themselves, the risk associated with lending to those companies." The narrator concludes by stating that "they began selling derivatives that were simply bets on any and all..." The atmosphere shifts between the calm, controlled interview settings and the chaotic, data-driven trading floor, highlighting the tension between analysis and action in the financial world.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a computer screen displaying financial data, including columns for 'POSITION', 'DURATION', and 'MANAG', as a male narrator's voice explains the concept of managing 'portfolios, whether the bank owned them or not'. The scene quickly shifts to a blurred shot of a man in a white shirt intensely focused on a screen, while the narrator continues, detailing how 'These products came to be known as synthetic collateralized debt obligations, synthetic CDOs'. Terri Duhon, a woman with dark hair and a long beaded necklace, then appears in a dimly lit room, speaking expressively and gesturing with her hands, explaining that 'There were investors who were able to invest in some entities that they had not had access to before'. A man in a dark blazer and light blue shirt interjects, stating this was achieved 'by buying a credit default swap'. Terri Duhon returns, continuing her explanation with animated hand movements, elaborating on how 'investing in a credit default swap' provided access, leading to 'a lot of very positive reinforcement of the market and it just grew, it grew very naturally'. As she concludes, 'Once the seed was planted, there wasn't any stopping it', the scene transitions to a bustling trading floor with the ambient sounds of activity, showing a man covering his mouth in apparent shock and another intently focused on multiple screens. The male narrator's voice returns, describing this as 'the beginning of an unfettered brave new world of banking'. The man in the dark blazer then comments, 'This was pretty new stuff', before Terri Duhon reappears, now smiling warmly, and enthusiastically reiterates, 'This was... this was incredibly new stuff. It was amazing. It was clearly a product that was in need, we had identified a need'. The video concludes with a group photo of seven professionals, including Terri Duhon, smiling for the camera, as the narrator mentions 'Most of the members of the global derivatives group at JP Morgan were in their...'.

The segment concludes with a return to Blythe Masters, reinforcing her role as a key commentator on the financial industry.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a high-angle, blurred shot of a bustling trading floor, filled with desks, multiple computer monitors, and traders intensely focused on their screens, establishing a chaotic, data-driven atmosphere, as a calm, authoritative male narrator explains how people "got excited about the money to be made writing credit derivatives." Tense, building background music plays throughout, accompanied by the sound of a passing car and a whoosh transition. The camera then shifts to a close-up of the 'Morgan Stanley' logo on a glass door, with people in business attire walking through the entrance, reinforcing the corporate setting, while the narrator introduces "Paul LeBlanc, a derivative salesman at Morgan Stanley," who "remembers the pressure to get more deals done." A close-up interview then shows Paul LeBlanc, a man in glasses and a patterned blazer, gesturing while speaking in a calm, clear voice about how "the volume of transactions was just exploding" and how he "used to talk about it every meeting how this is a growing market." This is followed by quick, blurred shots of hands typing on keyboards and a trader holding a phone, emphasizing the fast-paced nature of the trading environment, with overlapping audio of a female news anchor mentioning "the Nasdaq getting a nice..." and other voices discussing making money amidst trading floor chatter. The camera then focuses on a wall of monitors displaying financial tickers, including the Dow Industrials and stock prices, with the news anchor visible on one screen, as Paul LeBlanc continues, stating derivatives were "a massively important sector for us to focus on." The narrator then adds that "it was a private market, unregulated and out of view," with the news anchor in the background mentioning "The Dow up just about two and three quarters." The segment concludes with a close-up interview of Christopher Whalen, an investment banker identified by a lower-third graphic, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit room with warm lighting, his clear, analytical voice explaining that "unlike an an exchange traded market where all the banks can see all the positions," there's "no public market for these derivatives," and "nobody else in the market knows what's going on."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of hands typing rapidly on a specialized trading keyboard, a Starbucks cup visible in the background, accompanied by the sounds of rapid typing and general office chatter. A male voice (Christopher Whalen) explains how the opaque market led to huge spreads in derivative pricing. An overhead shot reveals a cluttered desk with multiple monitors, a calculator, and notes, emphasizing the intense financial environment. The scene transitions to an interview setting where a second male voice (the Interviewee in Blue Tie) questions the profitability for bankers selling these products. Christopher Whalen, an investment banker in a dark suit and red tie, then elaborates, stating that derivative spreads are significantly larger than those for comparable cash securities, which is why banks trade them. The interviewee in a blue tie clarifies cash securities as equities and bonds, before Christopher Whalen prompts for a clearer picture of the profits involved. He then gives an example: while a bank might earn 1% on a 'very hot' IPO like Facebook's, a derivative security could yield ten times that profit. The scene shifts to a trading floor, where monitors display the 'Interactive Brokers' logo and financial tickers, as an unidentified male voice (Voice 3) mentions 'luxury' and a 'secretive banking system.' Christopher Whalen reiterates that a derivative deal of the same size could yield ten times the profit. A CNBC broadcast promoting 'Filthy Rich Investigations Inc.' appears, while Voice 3 continues to speak about 'the filthy rich' and 'the Palm Jumeirah.' The segment concludes with an overhead view of a trader intensely focused on his screens, calculator, and notes, as a fourth unidentified male voice (Voice 4) explains that the business created was 'immensely profitable,' but warns of an underlying 'problem.' Throughout, the ambient sounds of typing and office chatter persist.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an overhead shot of a Trader at Desk, intensely focused on his work amidst a cluttered desk with multiple monitors, a specialized keyboard, calculator, notepad, and phone, including a close-up of a monitor displaying the 'Interactive Brokers' logo and financial data. A male voice comments on finance, stating that while people assume risk can be eliminated, "all you can do is move it around from one party to another party." The scene transitions to another Trader at Desk in a plaid shirt, also engaged in trading, with a Bloomberg Markets magazine visible. A dramatic, slightly ominous instrumental track begins as the visuals shift to a wide, hazy view of Washington D.C., showcasing the Washington Monument and U.S. Capitol Building, framed by American flags and trees, followed by a busy street in front of the Lincoln Memorial with cars, pedestrians, and a 'SPEED LIMIT 30' sign. A narrator's voice notes, "There was growing concern in Washington." The video then moves inside the ornate Senate chamber where Sen. Byron Dorgan (D-ND) is speaking passionately in 1999, identified by a FRONTLINE lower-third graphic. His clear, firm voice expresses urgent concerns about financial risk, stating, "We are moving towards greater risk. We must do something to address the regulation of hedge funds and especially derivatives in this country... That kind of risk overhanging the financial institutions this country, one day, with a thud, will wake everyone up." The camera briefly shows the Capitol dome through a window before cutting to a hearing room where a woman with short brown hair and glasses, seated at a table with microphones, listens intently. The narrator's voice returns, mentioning "Proposals circulated to rein in the banks and to regulate derivatives," followed by the woman's calm voice asking, "What are you trying to protect? We're trying to protect the money of the American public, which is at risk in..."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens on a formal hearing, where a Committee Member in a dark suit drinks water while a Hearing Room Witness, a woman in a beige blazer, intently listens at a table with microphones. Her authoritative voice explains, "we are the regulator which has been given the authority to oversee the major derivatives markets." The scene then cuts to Dennis Kelleher, Chief Counsel for Sen. Dorgan, speaking in a dimly lit office, his clear male voice asserting that Brooksley Born was "absolutely right" about the need for transparency and regulation of derivatives to prevent a "massive taxpayer bailout." A brief outdoor shot shows men in suits exiting a black limousine on a city sidewalk, as a male narrator's voice states, "The banks lobbied hard for no derivative regulation." The video shifts to Frank Partnoy, Author of 'Infectious Greed', in a dark room with bookshelves, with a view of the U.S. Capitol Building through a window. His distinct male voice elaborates that "The banks didn't want anyone to know how much risk they were taking on," and "lobbied so hard to make sure that swaps and derivatives would be treated differently from other kinds of financial products." A close-up of Dennis Kelleher is shown as the narrator adds, "Others wanted them to be regulated like insurance," before Frank Partnoy's voice returns, explaining that "One of the most heavily regulated products in the country are insurance products for all the obvious..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Dennis Kelleher, Chief Counsel to Sen. Dorgan, speaking earnestly in a dimly lit office, wearing a dark suit and green-striped tie. He explains, gesturing with his hands, that while writing insurance requires sufficient funds to pay off claims, credit default swaps do not, and importantly, involve no disclosure. He emphasizes that these are insurance products designed specifically to avoid regulation, leading to capital and activity moving into these unregulated areas. Another man in a dark suit and striped tie listens intently across from him. The scene then cuts to an older man with glasses, in a dark pinstripe suit and red tie, seated at a hearing table with microphones, who states with a focused and intense demeanor that 'regulation of derivatives transactions that are privately negotiated by professionals is unnecessary.' A narrator then explains that 'The chairman of the Fed, Alan Greenspan, sided with the banks, hinders the efficiency of markets.' Finally, Simon Johnson, Economist at MIT, appears in a dimly lit interview setting, wearing a dark suit and yellow patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera with a serious and analytical expression, detailing how Alan Greenspan 'was coming from a very libertarian tradition,' advocating for a 'hands off' approach, believing 'the markets will sort themselves out,' and questioning what happens 'if there's a problem.'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Simon Johnson, an economist from MIT, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting. Wearing a dark suit, light blue shirt, and a yellow patterned tie, he maintains a serious and analytical expression as he speaks in a calm, analytical male voice about the Federal Reserve's operations for "almost 20 years." Ominous, low-key orchestral music begins as the scene transitions to a series of exterior shots of Washington D.C., including the U.S. Capitol Building and the Lincoln Memorial, framed through bare tree branches during what appears to be dusk or early evening, creating a somber and reflective atmosphere. An authoritative male narrator states, "On Capitol Hill, supporters of bank deregulation made urgent, stark pleas." The camera pans across the Capitol dome and surrounding buildings, emphasizing the grandeur and political significance. The video then cuts to archival footage from 1999, showing Sen. Byron Dorgan, identified by a lower-third graphic, speaking in the Senate chamber. Wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and a gold patterned tie, he is seated at a wooden desk, speaking with a focused and earnest, passionate male voice, warning that "The future of America's dominance as the financial center of the world is at stake." The narrator then adds that "Before them was legislation to lift restrictions on how banks could do business." The scene shifts again to another senator, an older man with glasses, wearing a dark pinstripe suit and a blue patterned tie, seated in the Senate chamber, gesturing with his hands as he speaks with a deep, resonant, and emphatic male voice. He argues that if "this bill" isn't passed, cities like "London or Frankfurt, or Shanghai" could become the financial capital of the world, and that "This bill is going to make America more competitive on the world market and that's important." The narrator interjects, mentioning "legislation to prevent oversight of credit derivatives." The older senator continues, emphasizing that the bill "affects every business in America" and "benefits every consumer in America." The segment concludes with a final exterior shot of the Lincoln Memorial, viewed through a grid-like window, reinforcing the theme of governance and national institutions, as the orchestral music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an expansive aerial view of a densely packed suburban neighborhood, transitioning to a high-angle shot of a multi-lane highway with swift traffic, and then a ground-level perspective driving under an overpass towards a city skyline, all set to an upbeat, slightly tense background music. A male voice (Voice 1) narrates about "levels of population growth" in places like Florida, Nevada, California, and Georgia. The scene shifts to an office where a man in a dark suit and red tie, later identified by Voice 1 as Roy Barnes, the former governor of Georgia, sits at a cluttered desk, speaking directly to the camera. Barnes, speaking in a deeper, gravelly voice, states that "Atlanta was one of the hottest markets in the country." The video cuts to a construction site with a worker on a wooden frame structure and an excavator digging into earth, as Barnes continues, explaining that "Georgia was the fourth fastest growing state" at the turn of the last century, and "the fastest growing state east of the Mississippi," making it "a hot market to start with." Barnes reappears in his office, smoking a pipe while reviewing documents, as Voice 1 notes his election in 1998 and his renown for taking on Wall Street over subprime lending, a market "the street had traditionally avoided." Exterior shots of corporate buildings, including one labeled 'AMERIQUEST' and another with 'Countrywide Home Loans' signage, are shown, visually linking to the financial discussion. Barnes is then seen using a calculator, before the segment concludes with a close-up of his serious, focused face, as he elaborates on the history of subprime, stating, "In the 80s, there was no place for subprime. Nobody wanted it. The banks wouldn't buy it because there was a higher risk," and explaining that "what really changed the appetite for subprime mortgage was you could securitize, and you could sell it on Wall Street."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a point-of-view shot from inside a moving car, driving through a suburban neighborhood with houses and trees visible, as a serious-toned narrator, accompanied by somber background music, states that big banks on Wall Street failed to see the "wave of lending abuses" happening across the US. This transitions to a close-up of a home equity loan offer document, highlighting high interest rates and payment examples, with text emphasizing 'No Out Of Pocket Costs!' and 'Quick Processing Times!', while the narrator continues, describing "The Wild West experience in home mortgages" and citing the case of 41-year-old Essie Mae Hector, a mother of three, who agreed to a second mortgage at 27.5%. The scene then cuts to Prof. Frank Alexander from Emory University Law School, speaking calmly and clearly in a warmly lit indoor setting, explaining, "We were creating mortgages that we had never seen before. And they were being created faster and faster." A woman in a red patterned top is then shown reading a document with an engaged and concerned expression, as a male voice reveals that "The interest rate on these loans was as high as 42%." An aerial view of a dense suburban neighborhood, showcasing rows of houses, swimming pools, and tree-lined streets, accompanies the narrator's observation that "borrowers [were] given loans that were greater than the value of their home," and "homebuyers were getting loans that had no income." The video shifts to a close-up of a man in a light blue shirt looking down, possibly reviewing documents, followed by Prof. Frank Alexander seated in a living room with his hands clasped. Georgia State Senator Vincent Fort is then seen seated at a formal table with a folder and water bottle, gesturing as he clearly and directly explains the compounding issues: "When you have a high interest rate, then you have high points, then you have prepayment penalties, when you have balloon payments, when you have adjustable rate mortgages, and when you layer those bad practices on top of a high interest rate..." The segment concludes with the woman in the red top and the man in the light blue shirt seated together, the woman holding a document, as Senator Fort's voice fades out.

A passionate male voice, later identified as the Man in Dark Suit and Red Tie, states, "It becomes predatory," and "We don't need rhetoric," as the scene shifts to him speaking at a podium. A narrator explains that "Housing advocates around the country took on predatory lenders," while the Man in Dark Suit and Red Tie emphasizes, "People are tricked into owing more money than they could ever dream." Following a close-up of the Georgia state seal, Governor Roy Barnes is seen at a podium, flanked by the same Man in Dark Suit and Red Tie, as the narrator adds, "But one of the fiercest fights was here in Georgia." FOX 5's Amanda Davis reports on 'Predatory Lending,' stating, "Governor Barnes and others are making a last-ditch effort," with the narrator clarifying, "over what was called the Georgia Fair Lending Act." Another male voice, possibly a reporter, mentions the bill is "up right now on the House floor," before the narrator concludes, "The mortgage lenders and the banks struck back." The Man in Dark Suit and Red Tie is then heard passionately declaring, "None of these people have a clue of what's going on. Nobody here understands the business. And they didn't let us speak." A man in a suit works on a laptop in a crowded room, followed by an interview with the Man in Dark Suit and Patterned Tie, who calmly reflects, "You would have thought I had uh recommended that we repeal the plan of salvation." Amidst a chaotic crowd in a grand 'STATE CAPITOL' hallway, Alan Lapides, in an interview, deliberately states the opposition's motive: "Money. Money." The Man in Dark Suit and Red Tie returns with a fervent warning: "This bill will cripple the mortgage business, it's going to cripple real estate sales, it's going to absolutely devastate the home market in Georgia. I can guarantee you." The segment concludes with the Man in Dark Suit and Patterned Tie, seated in a cozy living room, calmly explaining, "They were threats that the residents in Georgia wouldn't be able to get mortgages anymore because investors wouldn't..."

Morgan' logo etched on a glass surface, illuminated by a blue light, as a male voice (later identified as Bill Winters) states, "it made good sense." Terri Duhon, a woman with dark hair and bangs, wearing a black top and a long beaded necklace, is then seen speaking expressively in a dimly lit, wood-paneled room, gesturing as she explains, "We traded mortgages... but we had a lot of trouble getting comfortable with that risk." She continues, detailing how "The big hang-up for us was data," specifically the lack of historical data on "how retail mortgages performed during different business cycles." The visual narrative intercuts with a blurred hand typing on a keyboard and more financial data screens, including a candlestick chart tracking price movements from November to January, reinforcing the data-driven intensity. Finally, Bill Winters, a man with graying hair in a blue and white striped shirt and dark tie, identified by a lower-third as Co-CEO of JP Morgan Investment Bank (2004-09), speaks in an interview. He reflects on competitors, stating, "We saw that that UBS and Merrill Lynch had securitized products earnings that were growing faster than ours," and admits, "we couldn't figure it out. What we never imagined was that those other firms weren't doing anything at all." The overall atmosphere is one of high-stakes financial analysis and corporate leadership grappling with complex market challenges.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dark, high-angle shot of a dimly lit urban street at night, where a lone figure walks under a streetlight, casting a long shadow, as a male voice discusses 'taking the risk and sitting with it.' The camera then pans upward to reveal a modern glass office building illuminated from within, showcasing rows of desks, computers, and office workers, while a news-like male voice reports on 'sales of new single-family homes.' The scene transitions to a close-up of Terri Duhon, a woman with dark hair and bangs, wearing a black top and a long beaded necklace, speaking expressively and gesturing with her hands in a dimly lit room with wooden paneling. She explains 'the first wave of JP Morgan bankers who had developed these original ideas in the 1990s,' and how 'when they saw what was starting to happen,' other banks were 'applying them in ways that they'd never expected,' causing 'some of them [to] begin to get very worried.' During her explanation, the news-like male voice interjects, mentioning 'the biggest one-month gain.' The scene cuts to an exterior night view of a cityscape with snow-covered rooftops and glowing streetlights, followed by a helicopter flying low over a river near a docked ship, accompanied by a subtle, tense background score. Terri Duhon returns, continuing her animated explanation, recounting how 'we were just about to say done on a transaction,' and detailing a 'global phone call' where they discussed 'the risk that we were about to do,' having 'discussed it over and over and over,' until 'finally someone on that phone call said I'm nervous.' The video then shifts to a wide aerial shot of a city at dusk, with lights twinkling across the urban landscape, as the news-like male voice reports 'twice as many home buyers are getting adjustable mortgages,' with another male voice adding, 'a huge increase in new.' Terri Duhon reappears, emphasizing her points with hand movements, stating, 'We almost had stopped thinking and stopped reassessing the risk as we've gone along.' A male voice interjects, 'The reason is they are,' as Terri Duhon continues, explaining, 'suddenly we found ourselves with a product that was vastly different from where we started,' while the news-like male voice mentions 'an adjustable rate mortgage.' She concludes her statement with a thoughtful expression, noting, 'every little tweak along the way, we had all said...'

Until suddenly, we all looked up and said, hang on, it's it's not okay." The scene transitions to an outdoor urban setting in London, showing a man in a dark suit leaning against the stone steps of a classical building with large columns, as a red double-decker bus and other vehicles pass by in the background. A formal male narrator states, "The world is still living with a lot of big unresolved problems. Other banks were not so cautious. They aggressively sold subprime CDOs to customers all over the world." The camera then captures a busy city street with traffic lights, including a red double-decker bus, a white van, and a black taxi, emphasizing the bustling financial district. A close-up of a modern glass building reveals signage for 'Credit Suisse' and 'Bank of America Merrill Lynch,' followed by a shot of a red double-decker bus passing under a glass overpass with 'Morgan Stanley' visible in the background, as the narrator adds, "London became a second beachhead for their trading and sales operations." The scene shifts to a wide shot of a historic European town square with half-timbered buildings, a clock tower, and a statue, suggesting a contrast between old and new financial centers. The video then shows a modern office building with 'Deutsche Bank' signage, parked cars, and a traffic light, reinforcing the global financial theme, while the narrator concludes a thought, "and economic statistics turn encouraging." The final segment features Christopher Whalen, a man with dark hair and glasses wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and red patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit room with warm lighting and a blurred background featuring a globe. Identified by a lower-third graphic as 'CHRISTOPHER WHALEN, Tangent Capital Partners,' he explains with a clear American accent, "The City of London actually did Yoman's service in creating some of the nastier structures. They did this offshore. These were not SEC registered deals, these were all private placements, so they were going through legal loopholes." A bell rings, possibly from a clock tower, marking a transition, before the male narrator returns, stating, "A group of state-run banks in Germany, known as Landesbanks, were among the biggest customers." Finally, a female voice, identified as Desirée Fixler, who worked at JP Morgan, is heard saying she was "amazed by these banks' appetite for subprime mortgages."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Desiree Fixler, a woman with shoulder-length wavy brown hair, wearing a black top and a pearl necklace, speaking earnestly in an interview setting with soft, warm lighting. A lower-third graphic identifies her as 'DESIREE FIXLER, JP Morgan, 2001-04' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo. She explains in a clear voice that a core group of banks in Germany would "buy anything," and that her group believed they were "very naive," expressing amazement that they would purchase certain products. She elaborates that every salesperson was "envious" of those covering "Landesbanks and IKB" because it was "one of the hottest seats globally" for generating "tremendous profit margin," concluding that "they were big buyers." The scene transitions to an exterior shot of a large, modern stone building with a street sign reading 'Moltkestraße,' followed by a slow pan up the building’s facade to reveal the 'Deutsche Bank' signage in bold black letters. The camera then cuts to Josef Ackermann, CEO of Deutsche Bank, a man with graying hair in a dark suit and blue tie, speaking in a well-lit office with a city skyline visible through the window behind him. Ackermann, speaking with a distinct accent, states that "IKB was very convinced that they were one of the strongest bank in that area," and that "they were running around telling people how good they are in investing." The video briefly shows a close-up of the Deutsche Bank logo on a glass skyscraper, then returns to Ackermann. A male voice (likely an interviewer) states that "Multinational Deutsche Bank did several deals with IKB," and then asks Ackermann if he thought their products were "helping IKB" and were "good things for them to buy." Ackermann, gesturing with his hand, responds, "Yeah, absolutely, otherwise we wouldn't have manufactured these products and sold it to them," confirming, "So you were bullish." segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a man in a dark suit and tie speaking thoughtfully in a dimly lit setting, his voice discussing the mortgage market and subprime lending, noting they were not “overly aggressive” but “part of that market,” all set to a slightly dramatic background music. A female voice then takes over, describing Americans buying real estate in “record numbers” and how demand has driven prices, as the scene transitions to low-angle shots of feet walking on a wet city sidewalk at night, illuminated by streetlights, creating a moody urban atmosphere. This shifts to a wide shot of a crowded city street at night with silhouetted pedestrians, emphasizing the hustle of city life, while a male narrator's voice details how the “total outstanding value of credit default swaps” around the world was measured in “trillions of dollars” and “doubling every year.” Another male voice reports on “existing home sales” rising “4.5%” and setting a “new record.” The camera then tilts upward to reveal the towering JPMorgan Chase & Co. building, with a blossoming tree in the foreground, contrasting nature with urban architecture. A close-up of white blossoms on a tree branch frames a glass window, through which a man in a light-colored shirt is visible inside the building, working. The scene cuts to a woman in a black top and pearl necklace speaking expressively in a dimly lit room, followed by a man in a dark suit and light blue shirt, also speaking. The background music fades as Martin Smith, the FRONTLINE Correspondent, is seen holding a pen and gesturing, his voice asking if “top management at JP Morgan understood credit derivatives.” The woman in the black top, now identified as a speaker, confidently replies, “Yes, they did. Absolutely they did,” before Smith asks if “they at other banks” did, to which she responds, “No. Not all other banks, certainly not.” The video then transitions to an interview setting with Daniel K. Tarullo, Federal Reserve Board Governor, seated in front of a bookshelf with a serious demeanor. Smith's voice asks if “the regulators understood them,” and Tarullo, with focused intensity, states, “I don't think the regulators understood. I don't think the credit ratings agencies, the bankers, or the regulators fully understood all of the kinds of credit instruments that we're talking about.” Smith's voice summarizes, “In other words, some big banks simply didn't know what they had in terms of risk,” to which Tarullo adds, “Certainly, they did.” The lighting remains warm and professional throughout these interview segments, reinforcing the documentary’s serious tone.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sweeping aerial view of a densely packed residential neighborhood, showcasing colorful houses with varied rooflines and lush green trees, bathed in bright sunlight. As the camera glides smoothly over the suburban landscape, a male voice explains that "they didn't know some of the forms of risk that they had." This is followed by a female voice reporting that "Sales were higher than most regions, up more than 40%," and a male narrator detailing how "Housing prices continued to soar" and "Banks packaged more and more CDOs." A lower-third graphic appears, reading 'pbs.org/frontline More on synthetic CDOs,' reinforcing the financial theme. The narrator continues, explaining that "Theoretically, there was no limit. An investor didn't need to own any actual mortgages," and that "So-called synthetic CDOs allowed investors to bet many times over on someone else's portfolio of debt." The scene then cuts to a close-up interview with PROF. FRANK PARTNOY from the Univ. of San Diego School of Law, a man in a dark suit, light blue striped shirt, and purple tie, speaking seriously in a dimly lit room with bookshelves. He elaborates, stating that synthetic CDOs "allowed participants either buying or selling... to take their positions without being constrained by the size of the underlying market." The camera returns to broader aerial shots of the neighborhood, now including a red brick church and intersecting roads, as Professor Partnoy continues to explain that "all you had to do was make a side bet based on what would happen to this group of mortgages and have that be the basis of the CDO. The fact that someone had done it one time wouldn't stop you from doing it a..." The visual alternation between the housing landscape and Professor Partnoy's expert commentary reinforces the connection between the physical housing market and the complex financial instruments being discussed.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an aerial view of a suburban neighborhood, showcasing colorful houses and tree-lined streets under bright daylight, establishing a calm, residential setting, with a lower-third graphic for 'pbs.org/frontline'. This serene visual is accompanied by the calm, analytical male voice of Dennis Kelleher, identified as Chief Counsel, Sen. Dorgan, 2006-10, who, seen in a dimly lit office speaking with expressive hand gestures, rhetorically questions the difference between betting on a Super Bowl or horse race and certain financial activities, concluding, 'There is no difference at all. It's just a pure bet by somebody who has no economic interest in what they're betting on.' Another confident male voice asserts that 'the housing market's not going to go down at all, it's just going to go up,' followed by a third male voice describing 'the most phenomenal machine anybody's ever seen.' An upbeat, slightly urgent background music then begins as the scene transitions to a dynamic, first-person perspective ascending an escalator inside a modern skyscraper. This leads to a breathtaking panoramic view of New York City at dusk, including Central Park and the dense urban skyline, with reflections of people visible on the glass, as the camera pans across the cityscape capturing the golden glow of the setting sun. During this visual sequence, multiple rapid-fire male voices, sounding like news anchors, excitedly report on an economic boom: 'New homes are selling at the second highest rate on record,' a 'housing boom,' and 'Profits soared 93%,' with expectations to 'dole out $36 billion in bonuses this year.' The visuals continue to emphasize financial power with a close-up of the grand neoclassical New York Stock Exchange building, the exteriors of Morgan Stanley and Bear Stearns, and a low-angle shot of the iconic Charging Bull statue. As the music fades, city ambient sounds like traffic and distant horns emerge, and Gillian Tett, seen in a well-lit room with a bookshelf behind her, speaking animatedly with hand gestures, reflects, 'Everyone was high-fiving. It seemed to be brilliant,' and analytically explains, 'The combination of free markets, innovation, and globalization appeared to have delivered this incredibly heady cocktail of tremendous growth.' The final shots include towering glass skyscrapers from a low-angle perspective, highlighting their imposing facades against a clear blue sky, reinforcing the theme of financial power and urban grandeur, while a male voice mentions that 'Top executives will earn as much as 20 to 50 million.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with sweeping aerial and low-angle shots of towering skyscrapers in a dense urban financial district, emphasizing the verticality and scale of the cityscape. The camera glides between buildings, capturing reflections in glass facades and the imposing architecture of institutions like Wells Fargo, whose sign is briefly visible. A high-angle view of a narrow street transitions to a blurred interior of a dimly lit office, setting a corporate tone. A male narrator introduces Dick Kovacevich, former CEO of Wells Fargo (1998–07), as the scene cuts to him speaking in an interview setting, identified by a FRONTLINE lower-third graphic. Kovacevich, dressed in a dark suit with a striped shirt and green patterned tie, speaks with a calm, measured voice, recalling meetings with bankers and regulators like Chairman Bernanke. He describes how he would warn them, stating, "this is toxic waste... we're building a bubble... we're not going to like the outcome." The camera alternates between Kovacevich and an interviewer, a man in a dark suit and blue striped tie, who listens intently before asking, "What did your fellow bankers say to you when you told them that you thought this stuff was toxic?" Kovacevich responds, explaining that "the ones that were in it, said I was wrong. And everything is everything's fine. We don't see any losses occurring in this." Throughout the interview, snippets of other male voices, possibly from news reports, interject with financial commentary, mentioning "5 billion dollars in compensation," "sales of new single-family homes," and asserting, "But we saw risk all over the place." The sequence concludes with a blurred, fast-moving cityscape shot, followed by a man in a light blue shirt presenting a financial graph on a large screen, reinforcing the economic analysis context.

The video then shows a close-up of office windows at night, where individuals are seen working, followed by a bustling city street with bright billboards, including a large American flag display, and a blurred shot of traffic lights. The male narrator's voice returns, stating, "As interest rates rose early this year, home sales slowed and after years of record appreciation..." and mentioning that "the cost of borrowing is going up." The final frames depict a group of formally dressed people exiting a building at night, suggesting a corporate or financial event, with the city’s vibrant nightlife continuing in the background, as the dramatic music persists.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a nighttime street scene outside a high-end establishment, where formally dressed individuals exit through revolving glass doors, capturing the bustling urban environment with blurred motion from passing vehicles and pedestrians. A narrator's voice states, "The unravelling began in late 2006." The bustling city scene continues as a news anchor's voice reports, "Big trouble for millions of American homeowners." The narrator continues, "When housing prices started to drop, only a very few bankers could see the bubble they were trapped in," followed by a news anchor stating, "The market has turned some mortgages into time bombs." The scene transitions to a close-up of Satyajit Das, identified by a lower-third graphic as 'Author, Traders, Guns and Money', who speaks animatedly, gesturing with his hands, explaining in a calm, clear voice, "By 2007, 2008, all the smart money knew the game had ended, and all the banks tried to effectively repackage what they were stuck with as quickly as possible and get it off their books." Das continues, "But there was a second parallel movement which was going on, which was all about, how can we take advantage of?" The video then cuts to a daytime shot of a crowded city sidewalk, followed by a low-angle view of towering skyscrapers against a clear blue sky, conveying the scale and power of the financial district. A news anchor's voice reports, "The Dow Jones average seemed in free fall, ending the day down..." as the visual sequence continues with a blue-tinted montage featuring text overlays like 'Managing Risk' and 'Client focused'. The narrator then adds, "One of the Wall Street banks that took advantage of a declining market was Goldman Sachs." A news anchor's voice continues, "According to a congressional investigation, the bank created a series of CDOs containing toxic subprime and then sold them to customers." The visual narrative moves from the gritty realism of city life to polished corporate messaging, as Lloyd C. Blankfein, Chairman & CEO of Goldman Sachs, appears, speaking directly to the camera in a professional setting with a cityscape backdrop, stating in a confident voice, "We at Goldman Sachs distinguish ourselves by our ability to get things done on behalf of our clients."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man in a dark blazer and light blue shirt, speaking intently in a dimly lit interview, asking if there was an obligation to warn people that "the markets are going down, maybe you should stop buying this crap." The scene cuts to a woman with shoulder-length wavy brown hair, wearing a black blazer and pearl necklace, who responds with animated expressions, clarifying that while "crap wasn't used," discussions about a "temporary glitch" in an overall bull market did happen, with the belief that "it will recover. It has to recover." The narrative then shifts to a financial candlestick chart labeled 'Daily QCHF=' displaying market fluctuations from November to January, transitioning to a news studio where a man in a brown blazer gestures toward a screen showing a city skyline with 'wirtschaft' (economy). A German male voice reports on the German banking group, followed by an English voice-over stating that "In July 2007, the German bank IKB, stuffed with subprime, was the first bank to fail," as a stock graph for 'IKB Deutsche Industriebank' appears, showing a sharp 18% drop on 30.07.2007. The scene moves to an exterior shot of the New York Stock Exchange building with American flags waving, followed by a close-up of a flag fluttering, while a male voice reports that "thousands of homeowners are defaulting," and it was "only a matter of time before the crisis came back to Wall Street," potentially hurting home values. The final shot is an interview with David Wessel, identified as 'DAVID WESSEL, The Wall Street Journal,' who, wearing glasses, a white beard, a brown suit, and a patterned tie, speaks in a dimly lit setting with a 'FRONTLINE' logo, explaining that while they knew the housing bubble had burst and were reassured the problem was contained, by early 2008, it was clear it was "a much, much bigger problem."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Daniel K. Tarullo, a man with white hair wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and patterned tie, speaking earnestly in a dimly lit room with bookshelves. He speaks calmly and clearly about a "broad misperception of the risk in housing prices," specifically "the widespread view that we could have a regional decline in housing prices, but never a national decline in housing prices, proved to be horribly wrong." The camera pulls back to reveal his full upper body as he gestures, with a lower-third graphic identifying him as 'DANIEL K. TARULLO, Federal Reserve Board Governor, 2009–present' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo. A tense, dramatic background music begins as the scene transitions to a dark, first-person view from inside a car driving through a tunnel, accompanied by the sound of the car and overlapping, urgent-sounding news/financial commentary from multiple male voices discussing "difficult time in mortgage business" and "trade." The PBS logo appears briefly. The car then emerges into bright daylight on a city street, and the camera pans upward to capture towering skyscrapers against a blue sky, including a clear shot of the Citibank logo on a building. The scene cuts to Frank Partnoy, a bald man with glasses, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and blue tie, speaking clearly and calmly in a dimly lit room with bookshelves behind him. He states, "In New York, banks were trying to unload what they could. But there was confusion. At Citigroup, they were running in circles." A lower-third graphic identifies him as 'FRANK PARTNOY, Author, Infectious Greed'. The video then returns to an upward-angled shot of the Citibank building and surrounding skyscrapers, with American flags waving in the wind, emphasizing the financial district’s imposing architecture, as Partnoy continues to explain that "although it was tossing these risks off its balance sheet, those risks came right back, almost like a boomerang," and that they "had set up one business to offload risk and then completely reversed that business, taking those risks back onto its balance sheet." The 'FRONTLINE' logo remains visible throughout the interview segments, and the dramatic background music persists.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an ominous, low-frequency background music as a low-angle shot captures towering skyscrapers, including a Citibank sign and waving American flags, under a partly cloudy sky. Daniel K. Tarullo, a man with white hair in a dark suit, then speaks in a dimly lit room with bookshelves, stating in a calm, authoritative voice that large financial institutions lacked the management information systems to know their risks, a fact he found "astounding" when prompted by an unseen interviewer. The scene cuts to another interviewee, a man with dark hair in a gray suit, who speaks with a clear, slightly urgent tone about "the origination of these subprime loans, the creation of the CDOs," declaring "that business is gone." The video returns to the cityscape, panning upward through narrow streets between high-rise buildings, as a male narrator's authoritative voice explains that "it would all come down to those credit default swaps," questioning if "they pay off as they were designed to do." A new interviewee, a man with a white beard and glasses in a brown suit, then appears, speaking directly to the camera in a softly lit room. He thoughtfully explains that "banks are susceptible to runs" and "can't function if everybody comes and wants their money at the same moment." The background music intensifies as rapid-fire news clips mention "Merrill Lynch devastated by losses" and "Lehman Brothers," with a voice suggesting "it's time to take a closer look at AIG." The scene shifts to an aerial view of a sprawling urban landscape at dusk, then to a man in a dark suit walking briskly past the American International Building. The narrator's voice concludes, stating that "this time, it would be a run on an insurance company," and that "AIG was on the hook for $440 billion worth of credit default swaps."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Daniel K. Tarullo, a Federal Reserve Board Governor, in a dark suit against a bookshelf background, explaining the need for banks to have a "balanced book or nearly enough capital to back their losses." He is followed by Christopher Whalen of Tangent Capital Partners, who, wearing a dark suit and red tie, questions whether "everybody knew that AIG was holding a lot of CDS?" An interviewee in a dark suit and blue striped tie then emphatically responds, "No, there was no disclosure. That that's the whole point," continuing to assert that "the banks turn this market into their own private game" because "nobody knows" what's truly happening. As a tense, atmospheric score begins to build, the scene transitions to a bustling trading floor, captured in dynamic shots of computer monitors, intensely focused traders in white shirts and glasses, and wide views of desks cluttered with multiple screens, telephones, and small American flags, all contributing to a chaotic energy filled with rapid communication and the cacophony of keyboard clicks and indistinct chatter. The video returns to Satyajit Das, animatedly gesturing in a dimly lit room, who describes the situation as "a financial shell game" where "the risk was not leaving the banking system" and "everybody in the world was connected through these chains." The segment then cuts back to the high-pressure, data-driven trading floor, showing close-ups of screens and tangled cables, before concluding with a wide view of the active traders, reinforcing the intensity and scale of financial operations, all underscored by the persistent, driving background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a fast-paced, blurred shot of a trading floor, showcasing digital tickers displaying stock prices like 'Walmart Stores Inc' and 'The Walt Disney Company' in red text, indicating a dynamic financial environment. A calm, authoritative male voice narrates about "risk" and how "if any part of that chain breaks down... the entire system implodes," set against a backdrop of somber, atmospheric background music. The visuals then cut to a close-up of a computer monitor showing detailed financial data, including commodity prices and world equity indices with red and green numbers, timestamped 14:57. This transitions to a red LED ticker displaying 'JPMorgan Chase & Co' and 'Pfizer Inc' with their respective stock prices and downward changes, reinforcing the theme of real-time financial data. The male narrator's voice returns, explaining, "The idea dreamt up by a group of young JP Morgan bankers at a weekend retreat many years ago was supposed to reduce risk." The scene shifts to a group photo of seven smiling professionals in formal business attire, including Terri Duhon, suggesting a corporate team or event. The video then cuts to a close-up of Gillian Tett, a woman in an orange top, speaking directly to the camera in an interview setting. With a clear, articulate British accent, she describes how "Their original idea had been taken and it turned into a Frankenstein monster, which they never dreamt would become so big and spin out of control to that degree." A still image of Terri Duhon in a pinstripe suit, looking serious and focused, is shown. The segment concludes with a close-up of Blythe Masters, wearing a white blazer and a pink scarf, speaking with a serious American accent, reflecting on "a very scary time" where they were "in totally new territory," and the "absolutely unprecedented" implications of "Lehman Brothers could be filing for bankruptcy and AIG could be at risk of the same fate." The background music continues throughout, underscoring the transition from chaotic market visuals to personal, reflective interviews on financial systems.
